data.title: EURO STOXX 50 Index Ends 0.43% Higher at 5017.91 — Data Talk
data.description:
The EURO STOXX 50 Index is up 21.52 points or 0.43% today to 5017.91

  • Up seven of the past 10 trading days
  • Off 8.17% from its record close of 5464.43 hit Monday, March 6, 2000
  • Highest closing value since Monday, Oct. 14, 2024
  • Off 1.63% from its 52-week high of 5100.90 hit Wednesday, May 15, 2024
  • Up 13.96% from its 52-week low of 4403.08 hit Wednesday, Jan. 17, 2024
  • Rose 12.96% from 52 weeks ago
  • Up 3.01% from its 2025 closing low of 4871.45 hit Friday, Jan. 3, 2025
  • Month-to-date it is up 2.49%
  • Year-to-date it is up 121.93 points or 2.49%

Source: Dow Jones Market Data, FactSet

data.title: Sentiment more bullish over Feb cargoes
data.description:
West African crude differentials are finding support from higher demand for February-loading cargoes, traders said on Thursday.

* Angola's state oil company Sonangol has sold all its spot cargoes for February, with its Cabinda cargo being the last to find a buyer. Angolan differentials are up by 50 cents to $1 a barrel for most grades versus January, a trader said.

* Angolan Girassol was valued by one trade at dated Brent plus $1.50 or higher, up from closer to dated plus $1.00 for January cargoes.

* 0n Nigerian grades, sentiment was also more bullish, even though around 10-15 cargoes were still unsold from Nigeria's January loading schedule. One trader said values have risen, without citing evidence.

* Late-January and early-February supplies of Nigerian Qua Iboe were last heard on offer at dated Brent plus $2.15.

data.title: Bank of England Monitoring Gilt Market, But Sees Moves as Orderly, Says Breeden — Update
data.description:
By Paul Hannon

The Bank of England is keeping a close eye on price moves in the market for U.K. government bonds, but they have so far been "orderly," Deputy Governor Sarah Breeden said Thursday.

Yields on long-term U.K. government debt have risen to their highest levels since 1998 over recent days.

Breeden is responsible for overseeing financial stability at the U.K.'s central bank, and said there haven't been signs of dysfunction in the bond market.

"So far the moves have been orderly," she said in response to a question after a speech in Scotland. "So far so good."

The gilt market weakness mirrors a recent jump in bond yields globally, particularly among U.S. Treasurys.

After rapid falls, inflation in many parts of the global economy has proved more sticky over recent months, raising questions about the pace of future rate cuts by leading central banks, which in turn have implications for the borrowing costs faced by governments.

"Of course we're monitoring what's happening in the gilt market," Breeden said. "A lot of the price moves reflect global factors and that's to be expected as markets react to news about what the outlook is for the fiscal position."

Cooling inflation and signs of weakening growth suggest the Bank of England should continue to lower its key interest rate, but at a gradual pace, Breeden added.

The annual rate of inflation has picked up in recent months, but is nonetheless lower than the BOE had anticipated. Another surprise for the U.K.'s central bank has been a weakening of growth as 2024 drew to a close.

"The recent evidence further supports the case to withdraw policy restrictiveness and I expect to continue to remove restrictiveness gradually over time," she said.

Investors expect the BOE to lower its key interest rate at a meeting of policy makers in early February, with further cuts to follow. The central bank lowered its key rate twice in 2024, moving at a slower pace than either the Federal Reserve or the European Central Bank.

Write to Paul Hannon at paul.hannon@wsj.com

data.title: Ipsos has been assigned Investment Grade ratings by Moody’s and Fitch
data.description:
Ipsos has been assigned Investment Grade ratings

by Moody’s and Fitch

Paris, 9 January 2025 - Ipsos, one of the world’s leading market research companies, has been assigned long-term credit ratings of Baa3 with a stable outlook by Moody’s, and BBB with a stable outlook by Fitch.

These ratings reflect the strength of Ipsos’ financial profile, its leading position in the market research industry, geographic diversification, comprehensive service offerings and innovation capacity. They demonstrate confidence in Ipsos’ strategy and growth potential. They will also enable the Group to diversify its funding sources, improve its access to debt capital markets and broaden its investor base.

The rating reports will soon be available in the Investor Relations section of Ipsos.com, as well as on Moodys.com and Fitchratings.com.

ABOUT IPSOS

Ipsos is one of the largest market research and polling companies globally, operating in 90 markets and employing nearly 20,000 people.

Our passionately curious research professionals, analysts and scientists have built unique multi-specialist capabilities that provide true understanding and powerful insights into the actions, opinions and motivations of citizens, consumers, patients, customers or employees. Our 75 business solutions are based on primary data from our surveys, social media monitoring, and qualitative or observational techniques.

“Game Changers” – our tagline – summarizes our ambition to help our 5,000 clients navigate with confidence our rapidly changing world.

Founded in France in 1975, Ipsos has been listed on the Euronext Paris since July 1, 1999. The company is part of the SBF 120, Mid-60 indices, and is eligible for the Deferred Settlement Service (SRD).

ISIN code FR0000073298, Reuters ISOS.PA, Bloomberg IPS:FP

www.ipsos.com

35 rue du Val de Marne

75 628 Paris, Cedex 13 France

Tel. +33 1 41 98 90 00

Attachment

  • Press_release_Ratings_090125_EN

data.title: U.K. Pension Providers to Benefit From U.K. Bond Yield Spike — Market Talk
data.description: The increase in U.K. bond yields to levels not seen since 1998 will boost U.K. pension providers' solvency by increasing the discount applied to personal liabilities, says Mark Capleton, an analyst at BofA Securities. "This should be generally speaking quite favorable for pension schemes," Capleton says. The yield on the 30-year bond or gilt, as they are called in the U.K., hit 5.455% earlier Thursday, while the yield on the 10-year gilt rose to 4.921%, before stabilizing in afternoon trading. The selloff has been more gradual than in October 2022, when the long-term bond yields rose on the back of the U.K. mini-budget under the leadership of former U.K. Prime Minister Liz Truss, Capleton says. Market liquidity is much more normal nowadays and the daily moves are more modest, he adds. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

data.title: Tech, Media & Telecom Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Technology, Media and Telecom. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0920 ET - The food-delivery sector's road to recovery won't be linear, Morgan Stanley analysts write in a note. Significant progress has been made in the industry, with companies proving they can be free cash generative, they say. However, the sector still faces challenges. German online delivery company Delivery Hero's switch to an employment model in Spain is expected to hit adjusted Ebitda by around 100 million euros in 2025. Meanwhile, Deliveroo and Just Eat reported 3Q order growth around 1%-2% points below consensus in the core U.K. and German markets. "The past few months have felt somewhat of a false dawn for a rejuvenation of sentiment in food delivery," the U.S. bank says. Deliveroo shares are up 0.9%. Delivery Hero and Just Eat shares are down 1.4% and 2%, respectively. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

0801 ET - Weak sentiment and negative news flow around NAND memory chips will likely put pressure on VAT's medium-term growth outlook, Jefferies analyst Olivia Honychurch says in a research note as it downgrades the Swiss valve maker to hold from buy. A DRAM memory chip slowdown in 2026 will add to VAT's woes, as DRAM is the company's second most important revenue driver via its three largest customers --Lam Research, Tokyo Electron and Applied Materials--. This should consequently limit VAT's share-price upside this year, Jefferies says. The investment bank expects VAT to cut its full-year guidance in March. (maitane.sardon@wsj.com)

0647 ET - ASM International should book a steady stream of orders from Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co. over the next four to five quarters, Jefferies analysts write in a research note. Order from TSMC, the world's largest contract chip maker, should help the Dutch supplier of semiconductor tools meet or beat order expectations, they say. Intel, Samsung Electronics, SK Hynix and Micron Technology are also expected to place more orders, the analysts add. They anticipate ASM International will report 800 million euros in orders for the fourth quarter. ASM International shares trade 0.6% lower at 590.20 euros. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

0626 ET - ASML Holding is expected to report higher orders starting in the first quarter compared to recent months, thanks to large clients like Samsung Electronics and Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co., Jefferies analysts write in a research note. The analysts expect the Dutch semiconductor-equipment maker to post orders of 3.6 billion euros for the fourth quarter of 2024. However, they say these should climb to a range of 5 billion euros to 6 billion euros from the first quarter of 2025. The analysts cited an increase in orders from TSMC as well as the prospect that Samsung might soon be able to supply its high bandwidth memory chips to Nvidia as potential boosts for ASML. ASML shares trade 0.5% lower at 720.80 euros. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

0608 ET - European semiconductor share prices this year will mainly depend on investors' confidence in 2026 revenue and earnings growth prospects, Jefferies analysts write in a research note. They note that demand in major end-markets like PCs, smartphones, general servers, consumer electronics and networking remains weak. "We believe the weaker than previously expected outlook for 2025 is largely priced in for most European semiconductor stocks," the analysts say, pointing to 2026 as a more important year for the sector. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

0553 ET - Talabat Holding's business is still growing, with many growth opportunities looking ahead, Barclays analysts write in a note. Delivery Hero's Middle East subsidiary Talabat has strong leadership positions across several markets, supported by a well-executed strategy, the analyst say. "Beyond restaurants, it has grocery at 24% gross merchandise value and broader retail at 1% GMV driving incremental users and frequency," they add. Talabat's business is very profitable, with further margin expansion potential, they say. The U.K. bank initiate its stock recommendation at overweight and a target price at 2.0 UAE dirham. Shares are down 0.6% at 1.63 dirham. ( najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

0516 ET - Telecommunication companies are well-placed for another positive year in 2025, Morgan Stanley analysts write in a note. The sector showed its strength last year, outperforming the broader market, the analysts say. "Over the previous 10 years, the sector was a consistent underperformer, with only three years of outperformance," they add. However, resilient pricing, declining capital expenses and the return of M&A activity will likely support momentum, the U.S. bank says. Shares in Deutsche Telekom, Swisscom, and BT are expected to perform well in 2025, though French telcos such as Orange and Bouygues look less attractive, they add. BT and Deutsche Telekom shares are up 0.8% at 144.90 pence and 0.5% at 29.24 euros, respectively. Swisscom shares are flat at 508 Swiss francs. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

0428 ET - The IPO of Delivery Hero's Middle East subsidiary, Talabat, was a major milestone for the German-listed delivery company, Morgan Stanley analysts write. It showed investors the hidden potential of the group, the analysts say in a note. "Talabat is a play on the structurally sound MENA market, with lower regulatory risk, a net cash position, paying dividends, higher margins and cash generation, all of which screen more favorably than the Delivery Hero Group," they add. Additionally, Talabat's margin strength and cash generation are impressive, placing it as a global best-in-class food delivery company, the U.S. bank says. Delivery Hero and Talabat shares are up 1.5% at 29.79 euros and down 1.2% at 1.62 UAE dirham, respectively. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

2138 ET - LG Electronics will likely continue to face challenges from sluggish demand and higher logistics costs this year, KB Securities analyst Jeff Kim says in a note. The South Korean consumer-electronics giant is also expected to keep wrestling with rising television-panel prices and slowing business growth for electric-car parts, EV charging services and robots, Kim notes. He cuts his 2025 operating-profit forecast for the company by 7% to KRW3.2 trillion. LG's below-consensus 4Q preliminary results and downbeat 2025 earnings outlook prompt KB to cut its target price for the stock by 8.3% to KRW110,000. KB keeps a buy rating. Shares are 1.0% lower at KRW85,900. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

1426 ET - The merger of Getty Images and Shutterstock may draw questions from regulators, given that they are leaders in the stock images market, Macquarie analysts Tim Nollen and Ross Compton say in a research note. A combination would appear to consolidate a market that's already narrowly defined, they say. That said, online imagery is already widely available through Google or other internet resources, and generative AI is poised to upend the imagery market anyway, the analysts say. Getty may be looking at the merger as a means to better compete in a media world being infiltrated by generative AI, they say. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1422 ET - Small modular reactor nuclear energy stocks sink after Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang said it could be more than 15 years before there are really useful quantum computers. Investors believe AI and data centers could fuel demand for nuclear energy that's developed more efficiently in small reactors, making the SMR industry particularly reliant on tech's performance, Wedbush Securities analyst Dan Ives tells WSJ. Even though quantum computing doesn't require nuclear energy, the pessimism in tech's time tables, which dragged quantum computing hardware and software company IonQ down 37%, is giving investors pause when it comes to potential near-term energy demands. Nano Nuclear Energy, Oklo and NuScale Power each tumble 11%. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

data.title: SOUTHWEST ANNOUNCES LEADERSHIP CHANGES
data.description:
SOUTHWEST ANNOUNCES LEADERSHIP CHANGES

PR Newswire

DALLAS, Jan. 9, 2025

DALLAS, Jan. 9, 2025 /PRNewswire/ — Today two longtime Leaders at Southwest Airlines Co. LUV announced plans to retire from their executive officer positions. Executive Vice President & Chief Financial Officer Tammy Romo and Chief Administration Officer Linda Rutherford will retire from their positions effective April 1, 2025.

"For more than three decades, Tammy and Linda have led our organization with vision, integrity, and an unwavering commitment to excellence," said Bob Jordan, President, Chief Executive Officer, & Vice Chairman of the Board of Directors at Southwest Airlines. "Both have managed the Company through highs and lows--and Southwest Airlines is better because of their influence on many of our most significant achievements. We are grateful for their contributions, which will shape our future for years to come."

Tammy Romo

Romo joined Southwest Airlines(R) in 1991 and has held many Leadership roles in the past 33 years, including Head of Investor Relations, Controller, Treasurer, and Senior Vice President Planning. She became Executive Vice President & Chief Financial Officer in 2012, and since that time, has been responsible for overseeing the airline's finance activities and initiatives, as well as Supply Chain Management, Corporate Strategy, Fuel Strategy & Management, Fleet Strategy & Management, and Environmental Sustainability.

Over Romo's career, her accolades include being recognized by WomenInc. as a Most Influential Corporate Director in 2023, named D CEO Magazine's Outstanding CFO for a Public Company in 2022 and 2014, Dallas Business Journal's 2014 CFO of the Year, and named in Institutional Investor's All-America Executive Team for Airlines. In addition, Tammy is a Lifetime member of the McCombs School Advisory Council and an Emeritus member of the Accounting Advisory Council.

Southwest will begin a search for Romo's replacement.

Linda Rutherford

Rutherford came to Southwest in 1992 as a Public Relations Coordinator after time spent working as a news journalist. At Southwest(R) , Rutherford has focused largely on the Communications space, including serving as the Chief Communications Officer for seven years before assuming her current role as Chief Administration Officer. In her current capacity, Linda oversees Culture & Communications, Internal Audit, People, Talent & Leadership, Total Rewards, Technology, and Southwest University.

Among Rutherford's myriad honors, she has received the 2023 PRWeek Women of Distinction, the 2023 Missouri Honor Medal, and the 2022 Institute of Public Relations Lifetime Achievement Award. She is also an inductee into the halls of fame for PR News Measurement, PR News Public Relations, and the Texas Tech University College of Media & Communications.

About Southwest Airlines Co.

Southwest Airlines Co. operates one of the world's most admired and awarded airlines, offering its one-of-a-kind value and Hospitality at 117 airports across 11 countries. Southwest took flight in 1971 to democratize the sky through friendly, reliable, and low-cost air travel and now carries more air travelers flying nonstop within the United States than any other airline(1) . By empowering its more than 73,000(2) People to deliver unparalleled Hospitality, the maverick airline cherishes a passionate loyalty among more than 137 million Customers carried in 2023. Southwest leverages a unique legacy and mission to serve communities around the world including harnessing the power of its People and Purpose to put communities at the Heart of its success. Learn more by visiting Southwest.com/citizenship.

(1) Based on U.S. Dept. of Transportation quarterly Airline Origin & Destination Survey since Q1 2021

(2) Fulltime-equivalent active Employees

View original content to download multimedia:https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/southwest-announces-leadership-changes-302347267.html

SOURCE Southwest Airlines Co.

/CONTACT: SWA Media Contact information - swamedia@wnco.com and 214-792-4847, option 1

data.title: Health Care Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Health Care sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0622 ET - The U.S. Food and Drug Administration's request for a label warning about Guillain-Barre syndrome risk on GSK's respiratory syncytial virus shot Arexvy could negatively impact vaccine sales, Bryan Garnier Research analysts say in a note. While a causal link between vaccination and GBS-- a rapid-onset muscle weakness caused by the immune system-- hasn't been proven, seven GBS cases per million Arexvy doses were observed, according to the FDA. "We believe the observed GBS incidence very close to natural GBS incidence in the non-vaccinated population," the analysts say. Arexvy sales already declined double-digit in 2H of 2024, following the narrowed recommendation to a smaller age range and more at-risk patients, the analysts say. Shares fall 0.04% at 1359.50 pence.(helena.smolak@wsj.com)

0123 ET - Akums Drugs and Pharmaceuticals is likely a good proxy for opportunities in India's pharmaceutical market, Ambit Capital analysts say in a research report. An increasing share of outsourced manufacturing and tightening regulations support faster growth of the contract development and manufacturing organization, which caters to the country's pharmaceutical market, the analysts say. The Indian company's well-established client ties, net-cash balance sheet and limited capital-expenditure needs for next five years would facilitate its efforts to build its merchant active pharmaceutical ingredient and exports CDMO businesses, the analysts add. Ambit Capital initiates coverage of the stock with a buy rating and a target price of INR840.00. Shares are 1.0% lower at INR619.05. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0004 ET - New Zealand's S&P/NZX 50 finished the day 0.8% lower at 12943.74, with healthcare and utility stocks leading declines. The retreat brought the benchmark index to its weakest close since Dec. 20. Ryman Healthcare posted its biggest fall among top stocks, losing 4.7%, while bank ANZ Group and Goodman Property Trust lost 2.4% and 1.9%, respectively. Utilities Mercury NZ and Meridian Energy declined 1.0% and 0.5%, respectively. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

2256 ET - Riverstone Holdings' growth outlook still appears positive, the Singapore Research team at RHB Research says. This is based on the recovery of global semiconductor sales and the company's differentiated exposure within the healthcare specialty gloves industry, which has better profitability than generic gloves, the team says. Robust chip sales suggest the global semiconductor industry is recovering on drivers including chip demand for artificial intelligence computing, the team says in a note. In the healthcare segment, the Singapore-listed glove manufacturer is likely to diversify product offerings toward specialty gloves to yield better margins. RHB raises the stock's target price to S$1.22 from S$1.07 with an unchanged buy rating. Shares are 0.9% higher at S$1.08. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

2220 ET - Southeast Asia's rubber products sector tailwinds appear largely priced in, with share prices in the sector gaining 40%-50% in 2024, prompting RHB IB to downgrade the sector's rating to neutral from overweight. While local manufacturers may still benefit from favorable operating conditions amid a trade diversion in the U.S., sales volumes could normalize in 1H due to earlier front-loading activity, analyst Oong Chun Sung and team say in a note. They add that earnings growth may struggle to align with the sector's high valuations, indicating limited upside. RHB pegs Riverstone Holdings as its top pick for the sector. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

data.title: Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Financial Services. Exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1219 ET - The increase in U.K. bond yields to levels not seen since 1998 will boost U.K. pension providers' solvency by increasing the discount applied to personal liabilities, says Mark Capleton, an analyst at BofA Securities. "This should be generally speaking quite favorable for pension schemes," Capleton says. The yield on the 30-year bond or gilt, as they are called in the U.K., hit 5.455% earlier Thursday, while the yield on the 10-year gilt rose to 4.921%, before stabilizing in afternoon trading. The selloff has been more gradual than in October 2022, when the long-term bond yields rose on the back of the U.K. mini-budget under the leadership of former U.K. Prime Minister Liz Truss, Capleton says. Market liquidity is much more normal nowadays and the daily moves are more modest, he adds. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

1112 ET - The Mid-Atlantic and Florida offer the best opportunities for first-time homebuyers, a Realtor.com survey says. Harrisburg, Penn., tops the list, followed by Rochester, N.Y., and several Florida cities including Villas, Lauderdale Lakes, and Altamonte Springs. These markets were selected for affordable listings, robust local economies, family-friendly environments, and investment potential. With national asking rents declining for 16 months, buying has become more appealing, especially where housing costs 30% or less of income. In these top markets, housing expenses range from 15.2% of prospective homebuyers' income in Rochester to 24.8% in Wilmington, with median listing prices below the national median of $416,880. The most expensive market is Villas, Fla., which has a median listing price of $236,950. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

1022 ET - KPMG's outlook for M&A activity into 2025 is improving among consumer and retail companies. Inflation is moderating and higher wages support muted consumer spending growth, while a more accommodative financial environment should allow for further interest rate cuts, KPMG says. "As a number of uncertainties in the market have been mitigated in the past several months, it seems that M&A waiting game is over," according to KPMG. That said, KPMG believes transactors need to remain patient and disciplined in approaching the market. Particular attention should be focused on deal activity aimed at business objectives, long-term thinking and developing a detailed plan to realize deal value.(sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

0922 ET - Merger and acquisition activity during 3Q reflected a decline in volume, but a lift in value, KPMG partner Frank Petraglia says in KPMG's latest report on M&A in the consumer and retail industry. The total number of deals declined 13% on a sequential basis to 505, while the value of the deals increased dramatically by 402% to $59.4 billion, driven by Mars' purchase of Kellanova for $35.9 billion. "KPMG is encouraged by the third-quarter deal activity as consumer and retail firms engage in transactions that strategically align with profitable growth," Petraglia adds. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

0743 ET - Canada's housing market saw a mixed finish to 2024, with the strong fall market rally losing a lot of steam as resale transactions fell markedly in Calgary and Toronto between November and December. Royal Bank of Canada's Robert Hogue notes the drop in Toronto rolled back all advances made since September, though the soft patch wasn't seen across the country and a recovery is still on track in British Columbia's Vancouver and Fraser Valley and transactions remain near all-time highs in Edmonton, Alberta, despite edging lower in December. The economist believe further interest rate cuts by the Bank of Canada will stimulate homebuyer demand in 2025, which will keep the rally going in most markets even if strained affordability and federal cuts to immigration restrain the pace. (robb.stewart@wsj.com)

0712 ET - Barclays believes that Nordic bank share prices could be driven by capital return announcements, 2025 guidance and net interest income in upcoming fourth quarter reports. It thinks net interest income consensus expectations are too high for Swedbank, SEB and Danske Bank, but believes consensus net interest income and EPS expectations for 2025-2026 are too low for DNB Bank. "We believe DNB is best placed into fourth quarter 2024 numbers relative to Swedbank and SEB." Danske Bank may drop its full-year 2025 net interest income guidance, Handelsbanken consensus dividend estimates are too low but fourth quarter 2024 consensus net interest income is too high, and Nordea consensus fourth quarter net interest income and dividends are too high. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0641 ET - Deutsche Bank is expected to launch a share buyback of 1 billion euros at its fourth-quarter results later this month, Barclays's Flora Bocahut and Krishnendra Dubey say in a research note. A buyback could be an important milestone for the German bank, which hasn't repurchased stock since it completed a 675 million-euro program in July, the analysts says. Deutsche Bank is likely to report continuing revenue momentum for the fourth quarter, especially in investment banking, according to Barclays. After negative surprises in each of the second and third quarters, provisions will return to more normal levels, the analysts say. Shares rise 0.2%. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0602 ET - CVC Capital Partners has limited room to surprise this year after a strong share performance since its initial public offering in April, Barclays's Michael Sanderson and Krystal Han say in a research note. The Amsterdam-listed buyout group is set to continue raising investors' funds, but it will take time before that translates into revenue generation, the analysts say. Moreover, consensus expectations for 2025 already point to a sharp step up in CVC's carried interest and performance fees, the analysts add. Barclays lowers its recommendation on the stock to equalweight from overweight and increases its target price to 22 euros from 21.80 euros. Shares fall 0.2% to 21.32 euros. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0519 ET - Partners Group Holding looks set to benefit from its exposure to the U.S., where opportunities in both investment exits and fundraising are expected to arise this year, Barclays's Michael Sanderson and Krystal Han say in a research note. "We see Partners Group as the best positioned in our private capital coverage to benefit from likely 2025 conditions and we upgrade our rating to [overweight]" from equalweight, the analysts say. Shares in the Swiss private-equity group could rise as higher transactional activity provides evidence of an improvement in conditions, the analysts add. Barclays also lifts its target price on Partners Group to 1,470 Swiss francs from 1,120 francs. Shares rise 1.8% to 1,321 francs. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0428 ET - The impact of lower interest rates in the fourth quarter sees a continuation of the challenging operating environment for Nordic banks, Jefferies analyst Alexander Demetriou writes. "On the macro front, economic sentiment remains weak, and we await an economic recovery in Sweden." Jefferies lowers its EPS estimates by 1%-3% on average across the sector on lower interest-rate assumptions. The bank continues to see better value elsewhere in a European banking context. Nordea remains its only buy-rated stock among the Nordic banks. Nordea shares falls 0.6% to 10.88 euros. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0323 ET - Local banks are likely to remain the "key driving force" behind further gains on Singapore's Straits Times Index, IG market strategist Yeap Jun Rong writes in a note. Concerns that banks' interest income may decline rapidly amid the Fed's rate-cutting cycle have eased, he adds. So far, U.S. data points to economic resilience and persistent last-mile inflation, which supports the case for a more gradual pace of rate cuts by the Fed, Yeap says. The narrative of higher rates for an extended period should continue supporting the banking sector, he adds. The Straits Times Index is 0.8% lower at 3856.17, but up 1.8% year to date. (amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0300 ET - Last year's natural disasters resulted in $140 billion in insured losses and were mostly driven by weather catastrophes such as hurricanes, thunderstorms and floods, according to a report by German reinsurer Munich Re. Last year was the third most expensive year since 1980 and an increase on 2023's inflation-adjusted $106 billion. Overall losses from natural catastrophes--including those that weren't insured--rose to $320 billion in 2024, up from $268 billion in 2023. "It is striking that, from a long-term perspective, non-peak perils are increasingly fueling the trend of rising losses, while peak risks like tropical cyclones and earthquakes continue to be a source of loss volatility," the report reads. Fatalities were lower, at 11,000 compared with 77,600 in 2023 and an average of 17,500 for the past 10 years. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

data.title: Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0915 ET - Oil futures rise after retreating yesterday from 3-month highs in a selloff driven by large builds in U.S. product inventories. "Some wind has come out of crude's sails over the last couple of sessions," Pepperstone's Michael Brown says in a note. "The near-term outlook hinges largely on the same factors that drove the market last year, chiefly the demand outlook, and whether the Chinese economy is at long last able to embark on a stable and durable economic recovery." Geopolitical risks are likely to limit downside for now, he adds. WTI and Brent are up 0.6% at $73.73 and $76.58 a barrel, respectively. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0906 ET - Shell's weak fourth-quarter trading update broke its streak of strong earnings but its underlying performance and cash generation remain solid, analysts at HSBC write. The U.K.-based energy giant said Wednesday that integrated gas trading is expected to be "significantly lower" in the fourth quarter due to expiring hedging contracts. The one consolation for investors is that the losses will only impact earnings and not cashflow, the analysts write. There is little read-across from Wednesday's trading update to Shell's peers given most of the negative elements were company specific, they write. However, the sector's earnings momentum could remain negative over the near term, they add. Shares trade up 1.4% at 26.16 pounds. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0837 ET - Shell will be able to maintain its share buyback rate and continue delivering returns to shareholders even if the price of oil drops significantly, UBS analysts write. The London-based energy giant has the safest buyback record in the sector given its low dividend break-even point of $36 a barrel, the analysts write. If oil prices were to significantly deteriorate, which the analysts don't expect, then Shell would be the last in the sector to cut buybacks, they write. Shares trade up 1.4% at 26.16 pounds. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0814 ET - Shell is undervalued by investors given it has the best buyback record in the sector and a strong balance sheet, UBS analysts write. Shares in the London-based energy giant are down 10% since July despite its restructuring and retreat from green-energy investments, the analysts write. Shell can surpass its $2-3 billion savings target by the end of the 2025 and has the opportunity to make an additional $6 billion in cost savings, UBS says. Shell's valuation gap with its U.S. peers has narrowed to around 50%, and it could fall further as its renewed focus on oil and gas assets delivers results, they write. UBS upgrades the stock rating to buy from neutral and increases its target price to 30 pounds. Shares trade up 1.4% at 26.16 pounds. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0544 ET - Repsol shares look set to rise as refining margins rebound, UBS analysts write. The Spanish energy major's stock has fallen 12% in the past six months and investors fail to account for higher gas prices and Repsol's earnings momentum, the analysts write. The analysts increase their earnings per share forecasts by 17%, putting them 8% ahead of consensus expectations. Repsol shares could be boosted by buybacks worth around 800 million euros in 2025, a UBS forecast that is nearly 150 million euros ahead of consensus expectations. A strong balance sheet and asset sales across should also boost shares, they write. UBS upgrades the stock's rating to buy from neutral, and increases its target price to 13 euros from 11.5 euros. Shares trade up 2.1% at 12.04 euros.(adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0503 ET - Eni's share price has been boosted by asset sales and buybacks but this means there is now limited room for positive surprises, analysts at UBS write. Since mid-June, Eni shares have outperformed peers by 8% but it will be increasingly hard for the oil major to beat market expectation. This could cap its share-price growth, the analyst write. Investors are unwilling to place high-value on green-energy investments and the absence of future oil price rises means Eni's earnings per share momentum might be stalling. UBS downgrades the stock's rating to neutral from buy. Shares trade down 0.1% to 13.66 euros. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0147 ET - S-Oil is set to post a 4Q earnings beat on better refining margins, Hana Securities analyst Yoon Jae-sung writes in a note. The South Korean oil refiner, controlled by Saudi Aramco, may have widened profit at its refining segment since October, backed by better margins and no inventory losses, Yoon notes. He estimates S-Oil's 4Q operating profit at KRW279.90 billion, beating the market consensus forecast of KRW178.90 billion. That would mark a turnaround from an operating loss in 3Q. The company had posted a net loss for a second consecutive quarter in 3Q. Hana raises its target for the stock by 12% to KRW95,000. Shares closed 6.4% higher at KRW63,000. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

0004 ET - New Zealand's S&P/NZX 50 finished the day 0.8% lower at 12943.74, with healthcare and utility stocks leading declines. The retreat brought the benchmark index to its weakest close since Dec. 20. Ryman Healthcare posted its biggest fall among top stocks, losing 4.7%, while bank ANZ Group and Goodman Property Trust lost 2.4% and 1.9%, respectively. Utilities Mercury NZ and Meridian Energy declined 1.0% and 0.5%, respectively. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

1500 ET - Oil futures give up gains in a technical pullback, with the decline accelerating after the EIA's weekly inventory report. A second straight week of outsized builds in U.S. distillate fuel and gasoline stocks offset any upside from a moderate drawdown in crude. "Crude oil may have hit a trough in this week's report, with the 'get rid of the barrels as fast as possible to avoid the tax man' season now over," Mizuho's Robert Yawger says in a note. Refinery maintenance may start sooner rather than later given the big builds in products "and miserable margins for the refiner," he adds. WTI settles down 1.3% at $73.32 a barrel, andBrent falls 1.2% to $76.16 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1422 ET - Small modular reactor nuclear energy stocks sink after Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang said it could be more than 15 years before there are really useful quantum computers. Investors believe AI and data centers could fuel demand for nuclear energy that's developed more efficiently in small reactors, making the SMR industry particularly reliant on tech's performance, Wedbush Securities analyst Dan Ives tells WSJ. Even though quantum computing doesn't require nuclear energy, the pessimism in tech's time tables, which dragged quantum computing hardware and software company IonQ down 37%, is giving investors pause when it comes to potential near-term energy demands. Nano Nuclear Energy, Oklo and NuScale Power each tumble 11%. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

data.title: Basic Materials Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Basic Materials. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0748 ET - Ivanhoe managed a strong finish to 2024, though the Canadian miner's production guidance for the new year was lighter than expected, says Raymond James' Farooq Hamed. The analyst says 4Q production at Ivanhoe's Kamoa-Kakula operation in Congo was better than anticipated, driven by higher throughput with the phase 3 mill development. Still, he adds 2025 copper guidance included a wide range, with the midpoint lower than expected. Hamed suggests the conservative range reflects the potential for power availability issues to impact operations. Raymond James retains an outperform call on the shares, but trims its target by C$1 to C$24. Ivanhoe was last at C$16.92, up 27% over the last year. (robb.stewart@wsj.com)

1943 ET - It's too early to start broadly buying Australian mining stocks again given persistent macro challenges, according to Citi analyst Paul McTaggart. "We continue to see the mining sector facing strong headwinds as global growth remains subdued and near-term commodity prices linger at lower levels," he says in a note. Key mining stocks like BHP and Rio Tinto look like better value after recent losses, "however improved valuation metrics are not yet a green light for investors to dive back in, in our view," he says. Australia's S&P/ASX 300 metals and mining index is down 0.6% at 5251.7. The index shed nearly 19% in 2024. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

1931 ET - Mergers and acquisitions are likely to remain prevalent in the global lithium industry, as valuations decline on expectations for lower lithium prices in the medium term, Goldman Sachs analysts say in a note. The lithium market should be soft again this year as a number of projects ramp up, say the analysts. They reckon producers will be focused on cost-cutting and production efficiency. "Over the medium- to longer-term, we continue to prefer briners over miners," say the analysts, adding that price weakness and new discoveries could make many small, low-quality or remote spodumene deposits less likely to be developed. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

1720 ET - Australian stocks are expected to start the day in the red, with ASX futures down by 0.4% ahead of the open. That suggests the benchmark index may retrace some of its recent gains, having enjoyed a solid start to 2025 with five consecutive days in the black. On Wall Street earlier, stocks swung between gains and losses. The S&P 500 finished the day up 0.2% and the Dow Jones Industrial Average added 0.3%, while the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite fell less than 0.1%. Before the bell, mining services contractor Macmahon said it got a A$463 million contract for an Indonesian gold project. Miner Westgold Resources reported a rise in quarterly gold output both on quarter and versus a year ago. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

data.title: Auto & Transport Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Auto and Transport sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0911 GMT - Shares in shipping companies are under pressure after U.S. dockworkers and shipping companies reached a tentative labor deal, averting a strike at major ports. A strike would normally see an increase in demand for space on container ships and raise freight rates, boosting the ship operators' earnings. Jefferies analysts note that freight rates have been boosted recently as retailers increase their stockpile of inventory amid uncertainty surrounding tariffs and the potential port strikes. "The supply/demand balance remains steady, but a soft patch could come later this year," Jefferies writes. A.P. Moller-Maersk shares fall 5.3% to 11,100 Danish kroner while Hapag-Lloyd shares fall 6.7% to 146.40 euros. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0437 GMT - Competition could intensify for China's electric-vehicle sector in 2025, Nomura analysts write in a note. Demand is expected to be weak in January and February given rushed orders and the rise in subsidy applications for trade-ins of passenger vehicles in the final two months of 2024, they say. The beginning of the year is also traditionally a weak season for the auto sector, and the early Lunar New Year this year may further weigh on demand, the analysts say. Additionally, Beijing released the details of the trade-in program much earlier this time, Nomura notes. It views that as a sign of China's supportive stance toward auto consumption, which will likely lead to heightened industry competition. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0341 GMT - China's extended trade-in program will likely boost full-year auto demand by 3.0 million units this year, Deutsche Bank analyst Bin Wang writes in a note. The extended and expanded subsidies for replacing cars will likely further boost overall consumption and the advancement of electric vehicles and hybrids, he says. The brokerage forecasts auto demand to grow by double digits in 2Q and 3Q. DB also expects some demand to be front-loaded in 4Q before the purchase tax rate on New Energy Vehicles, a term that encompasses full electric vehicles and plug-in hybrids, rises from the current 0% to 5% in 2026. DB forecasts China's passenger car wholesale sales to rise by 11% for the full year to 30 million units. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0207 GMT - Singapore Post's recent share-price movements signal a possible bearish reversal, based on the technical chart, Phillip Securities Research's Zane Aw says in a commentary. Its share price filled a gap at S$0.550 and encountered resistance upon a retest of previous support turned resistance, the analyst says. The moving average convergence divergence indicator signals a bearish divergence with a lower high formed despite the share price posting a higher high, which supports slowing bullish momentum, the analyst says. The share price is likely to pull back to retest S$0.505, which was the horizontal support level during the October-December 2024 period, the analyst adds. Shares are 0.9% lower at S$0.540. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

1846 GMT - With another labor stoppage at U.S. ports possible, analysts are looking at how a strike by dockworkers with the International Longshoremen's Association could impact food prices. As in October, the effect of the strike depends on how long it lasts. If it's more than a few days, perishable foods run the risk of rotting before being unloaded. 48% of fruit consumed in the U.S. in 2022 was imported, with bananas being the largest import, according to the American Farm Bureau Federation in a note. The AFBF adds that $231 million-worth of bananas were imported into the U.S. in January 2024, with 76% entering the U.S. through containerized East Coast ports. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

data.title: Brunswick Exploration Releases Exploration Results from Its Mirage Lithium Project
data.description:
Brunswick Exploration BRW on Thursday reported the last results from its summer drilling campaign at the Mirage lithium project in Quebec.

Highlights of the drilling include 37 meters testing at 1.14% Li2O in hole MR-24-87 and 1.15% Li2O over 23 meters in hole MR-24-89 extending mineralization at MR-3 down dip where it remains open, and a new interval at the MR-6 Dyke with 1.74% Li2O over 19.7 meters in hole MR-24-84 and 0.93% Li2O over 13.5 meters in hole MR-24-75 extending the dyke to the northwest.

The Hole MR-24-91, drilled for forthcoming metallurgical results, "reaffirms the thick, near-surface continuous mineralization" at MR-6 with 56 meters at 1.40% Li2O.

"Once more, our drilling continues to expand the known mineralized zones at Mirage both along strike and down-dip," chief executive Killian Charles said in a release. "I'm particularly pleased to see the wide, well-mineralized intercept in MR-3 where the dyke appears to thicken at depth. This is something observed across many well-mineralized lithium systems globally and Mirage is no different."

The company's shares were last seen down $0.01 to $0.15 on the TSX Venture Exchange.

data.title: Swiss Stocks Remain in the Green; Swatch Group in the Red
data.description:
Swiss stocks extended their gains on Thursday amid light trading volumes, with the US markets closed for a national day of mourning.

The Swiss Market Index was up 0.43% at the end of the trading session.

Switzerland's foreign currency reserves rose to 730.96 billion francs in December 2024 from 724.69 billion francs in the prior month, data from the Swiss National Bank SNBN showed.

In the euro area, seasonally adjusted retail trade volume ticked up 0.1% month over month in November 2024 following a 0.3% decline in the previous month, according to Eurostat data. On a yearly basis, total retail sales rose 1.2%.

On the corporate front, the Swiss National Bank saw its shares jump 5.97% after stating its expectations of reporting a profit of 80 billion francs for full-year 2024.

On the flip side, The Swatch Group UHR shed 2.99% as RBC Capital Markets downgraded its rating to underperform from sector perform and lowered its price target to 140 francs from 150 francs. The move is mainly driven by persistent structural challenges faced by the Swiss watches and jewelry manufacturer and market expectations on its earnings growth potential that analysts view as "too optimistic."

"We see further downside in 2025, partly driven by ongoing structural headwinds (smartwatch adoption cannibalizing entry price portfolio brands, lower value retention for high price brands in resale market vs peers) and optimistic consensus expectations shorter term (FY25E)," analysts wrote.

data.title: New Customer Success Index Report from Gainsight Reveals How AI is Reshaping CS Strategies
data.description:
SAN FRANCISCO, Jan. 09, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — Gainsight, the leading insights-driven, post-sales solution provider, today announced that AI adoption in Customer Success is shifting from experimental to essential, as more than half (52%) of CS teams are now incorporating AI into their workflows, using tools that strengthen early warning systems, automate processes, and provide richer customer insights. In Gainsight’s third annual Customer Success Index Report, companies noted substantial ROI, with 91% stating that AI will have a moderate to significant impact on their overall CS strategy. In partnership with Benchmarkit, the report highlights the growth, strategic evolution, and adoption of CS practices across industries in 2024.

The CS Index Report reveals that AI is transforming Customer Success and reshaping company strategies and program delivery. By automating tasks like data entry and churn detection, AI saves CS teams more than 10 hours per week. It helps teams uncover deeper customer insights to predict churn months in advance and enable proactive, scalable customer engagement.

For the first time, this year’s report also includes data from industries other than B2B tech. While 95% of B2B tech companies have established CS functions, with more than half (51%) having a dedicated CS Ops team, 62% of all other industries, such as healthcare, manufacturing, and retail, are increasingly adopting CS principles to meet customer needs and improve outcomes.

“Once a siloed department, CS is now a company-wide growth strategy,” said Brent Krempges, Chief Customer Officer at Gainsight. “Organizations across industries increasingly recognize CS as central to the customer lifecycle, driving collaboration across departments. This has made CS indispensable for recurring revenue models and extended its influence beyond traditional B2B enterprise tech boundaries.”

Additionally, Digital CS continues to grow, increasing by 15% annually, driven by AI and automation. Tools like online communities and customer self-service portals have seen a significant increase in adoption, rising from 42% in 2023 to 73% in 2024. These advancements enable teams to deliver highly personalized digital programs at scale, freeing time to focus on high-value, strategic initiatives. Europe showed an even stronger trend in digital, with a 2x+ increase over last year’s 27%.

Solutions like Staircase AI by Gainsight empower organizations to scale like never before by automating manual processes and delivering real-time, AI-powered insights across every customer interaction and channel. By harnessing AI, CS teams can focus on their core strengths—cultivating meaningful relationships and driving strategic outcomes that create lasting value.

Report Methodology

The Customer Success Index 2024 provides actionable insights for CS professionals, business leaders, and organizations looking to strengthen their customer strategies. Gainsight, in partnership with Benchmarkit, conducted the research from July to September 2024, benchmarking the CS organizational structure, key performance indicators (KPIs) like NPS and NRR, and the role of digital and AI in Customer Success.

Over 250 companies across North America and Europe participated, representing various industries, company sizes, Annual Contract Values, and geographic locations. Survey respondents included management at all levels, from C-suite executives to SVPs, VPs, and Directors, both within and outside the Customer Success function.

To download the full report, visit here. For a deeper dive into the CS Index Report findings, watch the webinar featuring Brent Krempges, Gainsight’s Chief Customer Officer, and Sabrina Leblanc, SurveyMonkey’s SVP of Customer Success, here.

About Gainsight

Gainsight, the world’s leading Customer Success platform, helps businesses drive efficient growth by unifying the post-sales customer journey. Its innovative set of customer success, customer education, product experience, and community management products are used by companies of all sizes and industries, including nearly 200 publicly traded organizations. With Gainsight, it’s never been easier to start and scale customer acquisition, retention, and expansion. Learn how leading companies such as GE Digital, SAP Concur, and Workday use Gainsight at www.gainsight.com.

Media Contact

Jocelynn Stidham

PR for Gainsight

jocelynn.stidham@gainsight.com

data.title: DXC Technology to Develop Ferrari's In-Vehicle Infotainment System
data.description:
DXC Technology DXC said Thursday it extended its partnership with Ferrari RACE to develop the super car brand's next-generation in-vehicle infotainment systems.

The two companies have been partners since 2021 when DXC started re-engineering human machine interface systems for current and next-generation Ferrari models, DXC Technology said.

data.title: STOXX Europe 600 Index Ends 0.42% Higher at 515.84 — Data Talk
data.description:
The STOXX Europe 600 Index is up 2.17 points or 0.42% today to 515.84

  • Up eight of the past 11 trading days
  • Off 2.32% from its record close of 528.08 hit Friday, Sept. 27, 2024
  • Highest closing value since Friday, Dec. 13, 2024
  • Off 2.32% from its 52-week high of 528.08 hit Friday, Sept. 27, 2024
  • Up 10.29% from its 52-week low of 467.71 hit Wednesday, Jan. 17, 2024
  • Rose 9.11% from 52 weeks ago
  • Up 1.51% from its 2025 closing low of 508.19 hit Friday, Jan. 3, 2025
  • Month-to-date it is up 1.62%
  • Year-to-date it is up 8.22 points or 1.62%

Source: Dow Jones Market Data, FactSet

data.title: Riley Permian Declares Quarterly Dividend
data.description:
Riley Permian Declares Quarterly Dividend

PR Newswire

OKLAHOMA CITY, Jan. 9, 2025

OKLAHOMA CITY, Jan. 9, 2025 /PRNewswire/ — Riley Exploration Permian, Inc. (NYSE American: REPX) ("Riley Permian" or the "Company") today announced that its Board of Directors has approved a cash dividend on the Company's common stock in the amount of $0.38 per share. The dividend is payable on February 6, 2025, to stockholders of record as of the close of business on January 23, 2025.

About Riley Exploration Permian, Inc.

Riley Permian is a growth-oriented, independent oil and natural gas company focused on the acquisition, exploration, development and production of oil, natural gas, and natural gas liquids. For more information please visit www.rileypermian.com.

Investor Contact:

Rick D'Angelo

405-438-0126

IR@rileypermian.com

View original content to download multimedia:https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/riley-permian-declares-quarterly-dividend-302347231.html

SOURCE Riley Exploration Permian, Inc.

data.title: Global Commodities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Commodities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1155 ET - Copper is likely to face near-term challenges due to the threat of U.S. tariffs on Chinese goods, but Beijing's stimulus measures to boost the economy should support global demand, according to MUFG analysts. "Copper is trading sideways with push-and-pull dynamics in play this year," they say in a research note. MUFG forecasts prices to move toward $10,000 a metric ton by the end of the year. Aluminum is seen as more insulated from bearish macroeconomic pressures. According to the analysts, tight supply in China is expected to push prices higher as the market seeks new sources outside of the country. LME three-month copper currently trades 0.4% higher at $9,085 a metric ton, while aluminum is up 1.3% to $2,540.50 a ton. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

1058 ET - A La Niña climate system is now active, says the NOAA's Climate Prediction Center. But the system is expected to be a short one, with a 59% chance of La Niña persisting through April. The Climate Prediction Center gives a 60% chance for La Niña to transition back into an ENSO-Neutral climate by May. While climate systems don't dictate how the weather will behave at any given time, a La Niña pattern during the spring typically brings colder and wetter conditions to the Plains and warmer and drier conditions to the south, meaning U.S. farmers in the Corn Belt may see ample moisture for their crops this spring. Most-active corn is up 0.1%, soybeans are down 0.2%, and wheat is off 1%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1030 ET - Live cattle futures are up 1.2%, with the contract rebounding from yesterday's selling on speculation that the rally, which had pushed futures to a new record-high of $1.96 a pound this week, was exhausted. "After nearly two weeks of big rally, cattle futures had their first material setback yesterday, trading sharply lower through the latter half of the session," says StoneX in a note. "Overripe technicals, exhausted buyers, and rumors of a packing plant closure all likely [contributed] to the move." The firm adds that the packing plant rumors appear to have been discounted by traders today. Lean hog futures are up 2.8%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1027 ET - Volumes this morning on the CBOT are thin, says Matt Zeller of StoneX in a note. "Overnight trade volume was light with the largest share going to corn, and the front end of that complex holding up the best due to having the best bullish fundamental 'story' heading into a massive USDA date," says Zeller. The January WASDE data are slated for release tomorrow at 12:00 p.m. ET. The U.S. dollar index is flat in early trading. The stronger dollar this week pressured commodities as a whole. The CBOT will close at 1:15 p.m. ET in observance of the National Day of Mourning for President Jimmy Carter. Most-active corn is up 0.1%, soybeans are down 0.3%, and wheat is off 0.8%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

0933 ET - Natural gas futures are higher with continuing support from weather-driven demand across much of the U.S. and high LNG feedgas flows to export facilities, while some freeze-offs limit production. Reaction was muted to last week's below-average storage withdrawal, with larger inventory reductions expected in coming weeks. "The overnight data remained frosty enough to where the next several EIA reports are expected to print larger than normal draws to decrease surpluses towards +50 Bcf, if not towards zero," forecaster NatGasWeather.com says in a note. The Nymex front month is up 1.8% at $3.717/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0915 ET - Oil futures rise after retreating yesterday from 3-month highs in a selloff driven by large builds in U.S. product inventories. "Some wind has come out of crude's sails over the last couple of sessions," Pepperstone's Michael Brown says in a note. "The near-term outlook hinges largely on the same factors that drove the market last year, chiefly the demand outlook, and whether the Chinese economy is at long last able to embark on a stable and durable economic recovery." Geopolitical risks are likely to limit downside for now, he adds. WTI and Brent are up 0.6% at $73.73 and $76.58 a barrel, respectively. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0748 ET - Ivanhoe managed a strong finish to 2024, though the Canadian miner's production guidance for the new year was lighter than expected, says Raymond James' Farooq Hamed. The analyst says 4Q production at Ivanhoe's Kamoa-Kakula operation in Congo was better than anticipated, driven by higher throughput with the phase 3 mill development. Still, he adds 2025 copper guidance included a wide range, with the midpoint lower than expected. Hamed suggests the conservative range reflects the potential for power availability issues to impact operations. Raymond James retains an outperform call on the shares, but trims its target by C$1 to C$24. Ivanhoe was last at C$16.92, up 27% over the last year. (robb.stewart@wsj.com)

0515 ET - Palm oil ended lower amid export uncertainties in Indonesia. The continuing concerns surrounding Indonesia's B40 biodiesel mandate and its measures to restrict exports of used cooking oil add to market unease, Kenanga Futures says in a research note. However, the forecast of declining palm oil inventories in Malaysia may provide some support and limit further downside pressure, it says. Investors will pay attention to Malaysia's official December production and exports data due Friday. The Bursa Malaysia Derivatives contract for March delivery closed 59 ringgit lower at 4,295 ringgit a ton. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0409 ET - Oil prices are edging lower in early trade after falling more than 1% in the previous session due to a stronger U.S. dollar and rise in U.S. fuel stockpiles. Brent crude is down 0.1% to $76.10 a barrel, while WTI falls 0.2% to $73.21 a barrel. "From a technical perspective, crude oil [WTI] is now in the medium-term bullish consolidation zone and should remain there above the $72.85pb support," Swissquote Bank's senior analyst Ipek Ozkardeskaya says in a note to clients. Prices had a strong start to 2025, supported by colder temperatures boosting heating fuel consumption and worries over Iranian and Russian supplies. Still, broader concerns of an oversupplied market this year and uncertainties over Chinese growth limit the upside. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0340 ET - Gold prices edge higher in early trade despite a stronger U.S. dollar, as investors seek safe-haven assets amid growing market uncertainties. A weaker-than-expected report on U.S. private employment for December provided some reassurance that the Federal Reserve may be less cautious about lowering rates. But the central bank's meeting minutes released on Wednesday suggested officials will hold rates steady for now, revealing they saw risks of higher-than-expected inflation partly due to President-elect Donald Trump's potential future policies. "That should hardly come as a surprise given that the median interest rate projection now shows only 50bp in cuts this year," Capital Economics' Paul Ashworth says. Gold futures currently trade 0.4% higher to $2,682.80 a troy ounce, with investors now awaiting the U.S. nonfarm payrolls report due on Friday for more insight into monetary policy direction. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

2312 ET - Crude palm oil prices could sustain their rising momentum and hover around 4,500 ringgit a ton to 5,000 ringgit a ton in the months ahead before easing, Hong Leong IB analyst Chye Wen Fei says in a note. Factors that could lead to softer CPO prices after 1Q include unsustainable palm oil price premiums, improved supply prospects due to stable weather, and potential delays in Indonesia's B40 biofuel mandate, she says. Chye maintains her 2025-2026 CPO price forecasts at MYR4,000/ton and MYR3,800/ton, respectively. The Bursa Malaysia Derivatives contract for March delivery is last at MYR4,247/ton. Hong Leong maintains a neutral rating on Malaysia's plantation sector, with IOI Corp., Hap Seng Plantations and Johor Plantations as top picks. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

2207 ET - Palm oil prices fall in early Asian trading, tracking soybean oil's weakness overnight on the Chicago Board of Trade, says David Ng, a trader at Kuala Lumpur-based Iceberg X. Palm olein's softness on the Dalian Commodity Exchange likely also weighed on palm oil prices, he adds. But Ng thinks weaker palm oil production could cushion the downtrend. Crude palm oil futures may find support at MYR4,200/ton and face resistance at MYR4,400/ton, he says. The Bursa Malaysia Derivatives contract for March delivery is MYR107 lower at MYR4,247/ton. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

data.title: FTSE 100 Index Ends 0.83% Higher at 8319.69 — Data Talk
data.description:
The FTSE 100 Index is up 68.66 points or 0.83% today to 8319.69

  • Largest one day point and percentage gain since Thursday, Jan. 2, 2025
  • Up for two consecutive trading days
  • Up 74.41 points or 0.90% over the last two trading days
  • Largest two day point and percentage gain since Thursday, Jan. 2, 2025
  • Up eight of the past 11 trading days
  • Off 1.49% from its record close of 8445.80 hit Wednesday, May 15, 2024

  • Highest closing value since Monday, Dec. 9, 2024
  • Off 1.49% from its 52-week high of 8445.80 hit Wednesday, May 15, 2024
  • Up 11.73% from its 52-week low of 7446.29 hit Wednesday, Jan. 17, 2024
  • Rose 9.81% from 52 weeks ago
  • Up 1.16% from its 2025 closing low of 8223.98 hit Friday, Jan. 3, 2025
  • Month-to-date it is up 1.79%
  • Year-to-date it is up 146.67 points or 1.79%

Source: Dow Jones Market Data, FactSet

data.title: DAX Ends 0.06% Lower at 20317.10 — Data Talk
data.description:
The DAX is down 12.84 points or 0.06% today to 20317.10

data.title: UK midcaps end higher but rate jitters persist, retailers tumble
data.description:
Key points:
  • FTSE 100 up 0.8%, FTSE 250 up 0.3%

The UK's midcap stocks ended higher on Thursday, rebounding from a more-than-eight-month low touched earlier in the session, as a selloff in bond markets kept investors on edge, while retail stocks were hammered by disappointing Christmas trading updates.

Marks and Spencer MKS reported a better-than-expected rise in like-for-like food sales in the Christmas trading period, but it warned of cost and economic headwinds this year, sending its shares down 8.4%.

Tesco TSCO, Britain's biggest supermarket group, dipped 0.5% after it maintained its full-year profit outlook.

The retail index (.FTNMX404010) dropped 1.3% to a near one-year low, with discount retailer B&M BME tumbling 8.5% after it lowered the top end of its annual profit forecast.

Greggs Plc GRG shed 15.8% after the baker and food-to-go retailer reported a modest 2.5% growth in fourth-quarter like-for-like sales, as tight-pursed Christmas shoppers indulged in fewer Festive Bakes, sausage rolls, and gingerbread lattes.

Still, the FTSE 250 index of domestically oriented stocks (.FTMC) closed up 0.3%, having dropped as much as about 1% during the session.

Midcap stocks have been pressured by a sharp rise in British borrowing costs this week on concerns about high borrowing in Britain and higher taxes on businesses planned by finance minister Rachel Reeves.

"The year ahead won't be all smooth sailing for the retail giants, as the sector gears up to battle imminent tax hikes," said Matt Britzman, senior equity analyst, Hargreaves Lansdown.

The benchmark 10-year gilt yields (GB10YT=RR) have spiked by a quarter point this week alone to their highest since 2008, while the 30-year gilts (GB30YT=RR) hit its highest since 1998.

However, a 0.6% slide in the pound GBPUSD supported the exporter-heavy FTSE 100 CURRENCYCOM:UK100, which was up 0.8% at a near four-week high.

Miners such as Antofagasta ANTO, Anglo American AAL, Rio Tinto RIO rose between 1.8% and 3.3% as metal prices edged higher.

Concerns about U.S. President-elect Donald Trump's tariff plans and stronger-than-expected U.S. economic data in recent days have fuelled concerns about resurgent inflation, prompting traders to scale back their expectations on the scale of rate cuts this year.
